data.title: Citizens Financial Group: Margin Expansion Continues To Drive Strong Growth
data.description:
A wider net interest margin, more progress on the rollout of its private bank, and a benign credit backdrop continue to kick in nicely for Citizens Financial Group (CFG), with revenue and earnings both growing double-digits year-over-year in the first quarter of 2026.

Rating the stock "Buy" last December, I liked the fact that much of this growth was essentially baked into Citizens' balance sheet. So long as interest rates remain relatively constructive, the natural roll-off of its low-yielding fixed-rate assets will be a multi-year tailwind to its earnings, while the buildout of its nascent private bank, previously a drag, had also begun contributing positively to the bottom line. With Citizens' earnings forecast to grow strongly on the back of these drivers, a then-multiple of 14.5x consensus 2025 EPS looked like a bargain.

These shares have returned around 16% since then, around 8 points better than the wider regional banking space. With that still leaving Citizens on a mid-teens multiple of trailing EPS, I still think the market is undervaluing its growth prospects. Accordingly, I rate shares "Buy".

NIM Expansion

Citizens earned a net profit of $1.13 per share last quarter, up nearly 50% year-on-year on the back of 12% growth in revenue. A widening net interest margin ("NIM") has been a major driver of this growth, and this has been a key plank of my investment case for the bank. While it isn't the only institution to have benefited from a healthier NIM, Citizens' dynamics have been exacerbated by its significant portfolio of receive-fixed swaps. These derivatives, which were taken out several years ago, essentially fixed a chunk of its floating-rate loans at relatively low yields, weighing on its NIM. The roll-off of these swaps now represents a multi-year tailwind.

Consistent with this, Citizens' NIM expanded by another 7 basis points last quarter, 5 basis points of which came from the impact of its receiver swaps. 0.07% may not sound like much, to which I would offer three counter points. Firstly, that's just the sequential gain. Year over year, Citizens' NIM grew 24 basis points last quarter. Secondly, Citizens has around $200 billion in interest-earning assets on its balance sheet. All else equal, an extra 0.07% of margin adds $140 million to annual interest revenue – a meaningful amount for a bank that earned $1.7 billion in net profits last year.

That leads me nicely to the third and most important point. As I have mentioned past pieces, NIM-driven revenue growth doesn't come with any incremental costs. It largely drops right down to pre-tax income, explaining why its earnings growth has been so rapid.

Per above, the tailwind from its swaps should continue beyond 2026, and management is guiding to a 3.30-3.50% NIM by Q4 2027. Given $200 billion in earning assets, you can see that this extra margin is going to add a significant amount to its bottom line in the coming quarters. Best of all, there is no real execution risk associated with this growth. It's just a matter of waiting for its swaps portfolio to roll over.

Private Bank Buildout Continues

Aside from its NIM, the ongoing buildout of its private bank was another reason I was bullish on Citizens. By its nature, private banking tends to be relatively capital light (Citizens disclosed a 25% return on equity from this business in Q1), making it an attractive avenue for banks to explore. Of course, it takes time to scale up a new bank from scratch, so this endeavour was previously a drag on Citizens' earnings.

A year ago, this business only kicked in $0.04 to Citizens' quarterly EPS. With loans, deposits, and client assets all up strongly in that time, its contribution has risen to $0.11 as of last quarter. This remains modest in the grand scheme of things (Citizens earned $1.13 per share in Q1), but the direction of travel is positive.

Credit Quality Holding Up

Since my last piece, we have obviously seen significant uncertainty come from the Iran war and fears surrounding the health of private credit. Citizens doesn't appear to posting any concerning credit trends right now. Net charge-offs were less than 40 basis points of total loans last quarter, down both year-on-year and quarter, and balances of nonaccrual loans performed similarly. Positively, management is guiding for net charge-offs to be "stable to down slightly" in Q2, implying that credit conditions remain benign.

Shares Still Attractively Valued

Following last quarter's print, Citizens has now earned $4.23 per share in adjusted trailing 12-month net income. With the shares trading for $65.23 as I type, this leaves them on a trailing P/E multiple of 15.4x (the chart below has not updated for Q1 results, which is why it shows a trailing P/E of 16.84). For reference, Citizens; ten-year historical average trailing multiple is roughly 12.

Of course, the "E" part of this equation is still based on relatively depressed figures, as Citizens' average NIM across the last four quarters was only 3.03%. It was 3.14% last quarter, it's going to expand by another ~15-35 basis points through 2027. Similarly, the private banking unit is going to be earning more in the coming quarters than it did over the trailing four quarter period, contributing to higher earnings.

With that, analysts expect Citizens to earn $5.18 per share this year, before rising another 23% in 2027 to over $6.30 per share. I repeat that much of this growth is simply a function of the repricing of Citizens' balance sheet, involving little-to-no execution risk. On a trailing multiple of 12x, in line with its ten-year average, that would put Citizens at over $75 in a two years' time. Tack on $4 per share in expected dividend payments, and today's buyer can credibly expect a circa $80 total return price, mapping to a circa 11% annualized return. With much of its earnings growth requiring no real execution from management, and with credit quality holding up nicely, I view that as attractive. Accordingly, I am leaving my "Buy" rating for Citizens Financial Group in place.

data.title: Tesla: The Stock Market's Valuation Laws Don't Apply Here (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) analysis, the stock price had declined from about $400 per share to about $350 per share before now rising back to around $400. Ultimately $350 was a good entry point, and $400 is fine if one is viewing this as a long-term investment and not a short-term tactical trade. That said, there are several operational developments that have emerged since that analysis, and the time value of a $400 per share stock price has increased. I will provide these updates in this piece and explain why I remain moderately bullish.

The image for this analysis is an artistic depiction of a Chinese fire horse, which is a metaphor for Tesla stock, which is treated as mythic while breaking the normal laws of valuation the way the horse appears to outrun gravity itself.

The Current Landscape

Firstly, I will focus in on Tesla's enormous latent industrial leverage. The company's installed annual capacity spans California with over 550,000 Model 3/Ys, Shanghai at over 950,000, Berlin at over 375,000, and Texas at over 250,000. This is against deliveries of just 1.64M. Management also says that early Robotaxi operations can lean on an existing network that increased Supercharging stalls by over 3,800 in 2025. In other words, there's an already-built physical ecosystem here ready to be brought into the AI and autonomy generation.

Admittedly, the robotaxi operating model is still early, but it's not theoretical. Driverless robotaxis began testing in Austin in December, and the safety monitor was removed from some customer rides in January. The Bay Area service is still safety-driver only, but several additional metros have been mapped for the first half of 2026.

Ultimately, it's Tesla's AI stack that will differentiate it in the autonomous era. Bears look at Tesla and say it's just a car company, but anyone who's gone deep into tech analysis understands that, from first principles, Tesla is an AI company. To be more specific, I feel comfortable calling it a robotics company shifting from a legacy automobile model. It is also diversified via its energy storage and generation segment, which rose 27% year-over-year in 2025.

Tesla uses vision-based systems and in-house inference chips to train its neural nets for Full Self-Driving and for its robotics. With v14 training on both customer and robotaxi data, the fleet can gain over 500 years of continuous driving data per day. Active Full Self-Driving subscriptions reached 1.1M in 2025, so the flywheel is healthy and continues to evolve. This is a remarkable data-intelligence moat.

Diving a bit deeper into the AI leadership position, Tesla's Cortex 1 AI supercomputer cluster in Texas is operating >100,000 H100s, Cortex 2 is under construction, and onsite compute should more than double in the first half of 2026. Tesla's AI5 chip is also targeting a 50x performance gain against AI4 and is planned for production in 2027. Moreover, Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF) is planning to manufacture advanced AI semiconductors in the U.S., which significantly reduces geopolitical risks for Tesla.

Valuation Laws Don't Apply To Tesla

If you don't like speculation, you probably won't buy Tesla stock. That said, I've self-trained religiously in the value investing school of thought, including all traditional variants of discounted value of future growth, peer-relative and sector-relative multiple analysis, and other methods of fundamental valuation estimation. All of that has to be understood, but once it is, you can throw it out the window in favor of making cash. In my opinion, an elite trader moves from asking, “What is this company worth today?” to “What will this company be priced at in X amount of time?” If you think the latter negates fundamental valuation analysis, you aren't doing it properly. But you have to break the rules.

As the famous saying goes:

“Learn the rules like a pro, so you can break them like an artist.” — Pablo Picasso.

Let's come back to the basics. Tesla has a forward P/E non-GAAP ratio that is 1,120% higher than the sector median and a forward PEG non-GAAP ratio that is 250% higher than the sector median. On the surface, you might reject a stock trading like that out of principle. I wouldn't blame you, but it doesn't mean it won't still make you money in the long term. Some stocks defy valuation laws and get away with it. Tesla is one such stock (and it's been doing it for many years). In fact, if you bought Tesla 10 years ago, your total return would be 2,300% today. How's that for speculation?

The only thing I'd be cautious of is that if you look at the long-term 10-year average in the P/S ratio chart above, the current price is substantially higher. That doesn't mean that it's not apt to invest in Tesla at this juncture, but it does mean that the company's valuation has no margin of safety versus its own historical average in buyer sentiment.

The caveat here is that the company's forward EPS long-term growth CAGR consensus estimate is about 37% annually, while its five-year average is 28.5%, meaning that there is a 30% increase in forward growth versus historical. This warrants an increase in the valuation multiples, but I find it unlikely that over a 50% rise in Tesla's P/S multiple against its long-term historical average is warranted as a result. In other words, the premium on Tesla stock right now makes it moderately overvalued even when using a nuanced valuation method that appreciates and acknowledges Tesla's long-term capability to defy the stock market's valuation laws.

I'm going to price in compression here to my forward estimate and forecast April 2028 revenue of $150B with a forward P/S in April 2027 of 12.5. If total shares outstanding rise over the next 12 months to ~4.1 billion (my estimate), then the stock price in 12 months will be about $460. The current price is $390, so the implied upside is 17%. If bought at the recent low of $350, the return would be 30%. As the long-term trajectory is strong, even with heavy overvaluation, I am reinstating my Buy rating here.

Risks On The Horizon

Waymo has over 500,000 fully autonomous robotaxi trips per week now, while already hitting over 20M lifetime rides. It's not the only competitor on the block, either, as Baidu, Inc. (BIDU) says Apollo Go delivered 3.4M fully driverless rides in Q4 alone while reaching a total of 26 cities (China speed helps). Tesla has the richer equity narrative and forward vision; there's no doubt in my mind about that, but rivals have stronger disclosed commercial robotaxi scale.

Energy is the ballast that can not only fund more of the autonomy and robotics ambitions Tesla has but also directly fuel autonomy and robotics scaling through better energy access. This is a dual win if the segment is developed strategically, and it is why I think it makes sense for energy to become a core segment for Tesla at this juncture during the pivot. In 2025, energy deployments rose 49% to 46.7 GWh, while energy revenue hit $12.8B and segment gross margin hit 29.8%, versus just 17.8% for the automotive gross margin.

The energy segment can help with the fact that BYD Company Limited (BYDDF) is a hard operating counterweight to TSLA sentiment on the EV front. BYD sold 2.26M EVs in 2025 versus Tesla's 1.64M vehicle deliveries, and in Germany, BYD registrations rose 327% in March, compared to 315% for Tesla. The price-war risk is not going to go away until Tesla has turned its autonomy moat into its primary operating advantage and real return driver.

Q1 Earnings Preview

Tesla reports on April 22 after the market closes, and with the stock trading high above averages, outlining operationally and fundamentally how the bridge from the current EV business to autonomy, AI, and robotics is developing will be key. This is far more important than beating current expectations, though that always helps. It's likely important for the investor base to understand, from simple first principles, where in the tech stack Tesla's long-term moats and distinguished competitive advantages are being established, as this is really what will sustain growth rates into the future and keep sentiment elevated.

Looking at near-term fundamentals, to hit Tesla's company-compiled 2026 delivery consensus of 1.69M after 358,023 units delivered in Q1, Tesla will need about 444,000 deliveries in each of the next three quarters. Hitting this target will come down to whether Q1 improvement in China and March strength in Germany are durable enough to meet expectations.

My final watch point is that Tesla ended 2025 with $44.1B of cash and investments and $6.2B of free cash flow. However, it also stated that 2026 includes six new production lines (Cybercab, Semi, and Megapack 3 are on schedule for volume production). However, if spending ramps faster than monetization, the bull case will become more duration-sensitive quickly, though I would view any pullback in this case as a long-term entry point.

Conclusion: Buy

Tesla stock is not for the faint of heart, but it is the kind of investment I would want to own for the long term. Ironically, Tesla is a kind of set-it-and-forget-it investment at this juncture, because the autonomy trajectory is massive, and trading in and out of cycles like I do is better done in other stocks. I don't own it, though, primarily because I maximize my annual return via rotation.

The price could be better, I admit, but you get what you pay for. Whether you like it or not, when you buy Tesla, you aren't just buying a company; you're buying a myth. If you don't like that, then step aside. You'll find plenty of traditional value stocks in other corners of the market. Tesla rewards the courageous and those who know how to think differently. I'm staying bullish, but I'm personally not buying unless I find an even better entry point ahead.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: America's Car-Mart's Crash Doesn't Justify An Upgrade At This Time
data.description:
It has been a rough couple of years for shareholders of America's Car-Mart, Inc. (CRMT). Back in June of 2024, I made the decision to downgrade the stock from a ‘hold’ to a ‘sell.’ This decision was not made lightly. After all, I hate taking a bearish stance on any business because it means that people, many of them good people, could get hurt if I am right. However, it is necessary when the fundamentals warrant it; otherwise, you are just deluding yourself. But I digress. Since I made that decision, the company has actually performed even worse than I anticipated. As of the time of this writing, the stock is down 79%. That compares to the 19.6% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. The good news is that management is taking steps in order to cut costs. But honestly, I don't see any reason to turn bullish or even neutral on it just yet. Until we can see signs of revenue stabilizing on a consistent basis or see a meaningful improvement in cash flow, I believe that maintaining a bearish outlook on the company is appropriate.

A Really Tough Ride

From an operational standpoint, I do think that America's Car-Mart is an interesting business. In case you are not terribly familiar with the company, it operates as one of the largest publicly held automotive retailers in the US. In fact, it currently has 136 dealerships nationwide. This is a space that I have a lot of experience in and that I like a great deal. But unfortunately, financial performance for the business has been challenged.

Some of this might seem surprising. I say that because there are certain things about the structure of the company that could play well to establishing a long-term competitive position. First and foremost, the company focuses on selling basic and affordable transportation for its customers, as opposed to many automotive retailers that sell high-priced vehicles. In the most recent quarter, for instance, the company, which sells used vehicles, recorded an average retail sales price of $20,634. That's cheap even by used vehicle standards. According to one source, the average price of used vehicles in the US stands at about $25,287. That same source says that, for a new vehicle, it's around $49,100.

Another positive about the company is that it typically operates in towns with populations of 50,000 or less. This means that, to the extent that there is not significant additional competition, the firm is able to establish itself as an important player in the automotive retail space in the areas in which it operates. Of course, this can be a double-edged sword. That's because, when new locations open up in those towns that might compete with America's Car-Mart, you end up with more challenging conditions than if the firm operated in more heavily populated areas.

Unfortunately, in recent years, financial performance for the company has been challenged. From 2023 through 2025, revenue has fallen slightly from $1.40 billion to $1.39 billion. Certain profitability metrics have worsened as well, though some have also improved. Take net profits as an example. Those declined from $20.4 million to $17.9 million. On the other hand, operating cash flow went from negative $135.7 million to negative $48.8 million. And at the same time that this occurred, EBITDA for the business expanded from $70.1 million to $101.4 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for the first nine months of the 2026 fiscal year compared to the same time in 2025. In this case, most profitability metrics for the company followed revenue lower. The weakness that the company has seen here has been the result of challenging market conditions. As consumers become increasingly under pressure, and as a combination of inflationary pressures and tariffs has pushed up the cost of vehicles, the end result has been less buying activity. When it comes to tariffs specifically, management said that the impact on the automotive industry amounted to roughly $560 per vehicle when it came to procurement costs alone. This doesn't even factor in other impacts that could be rippling through the space.

From 2023 through 2025, the number of vehicles sold by the business dropped from 63,584 to 57,022. Some of this was because of a reduction in the number of locations that the business had, from 155 to 154. But overwhelmingly, it was the result of a reduction in the number of units sold per retail outlet. That number fell from 34.2 to 30.9. And for the first nine months of this year, the 27.4 sold was down from the 29.7 sold, and when combined with the decrease in the number of stores in operation from 154 to 136, it's not hard to see that the drop in the number of retail units from 41,373 to 37,480 makes sense.

Unfortunately, on a same-store basis, revenue for the company has been falling consistently since 2024. However, one thing that management has done in order to improve financial performance compared to what it would be otherwise has been to increase prices enough to boost profit margins. While the average retail price per used vehicle has risen, the chart below that illustrates that the gross profit and gross profit margin of those vehicles increased also.

Unfortunately for investors, there are signs of worsening market conditions. The auto loan delinquency rate recently came in at 5.2%. You would have to go back to the final quarter of 2010 to see that number come in that high. What's more, management is reporting that the number of accounts that they have that are 30 days or more past due increased to 4.4%. That compares to the 3.7% that we see when looking at the previous year, and it's up from the 3.1% achieved for 2024. This is not surprising when you consider how high the average interest rate on car loans that it facilitates is. The weighted average rate, according to management, is 17.66%. That is astronomical. With the consumer becoming increasingly stressed, with consumer confidence levels near all-time lows, this is an untenable interest rate for many Americans.

Those who are bullish about the company might point out that the stock is trading cheaper now than it probably ever has from a valuation standpoint. Take, if you will, the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples of the enterprise. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean here. The firm is trading at just a fraction of book value and tangible book value. This means that, in theory, if you were to liquidate the business and achieve fair market value that matches book value, you would significantly improve your overall net worth in the firm. But there are some issues with this approach.

The biggest one is that the vast majority of its assets are tied up in finance receivables and inventory. Upon liquidation, especially if it is done in a hurry, those would likely sell for fractions of a dollar. Even if we were to see a 39.4% haircut on the combination of these assets, that could potentially completely wipe out shareholder equity. And with market conditions worsening, such a haircut would not be unfathomable. It doesn't help that the company has net debt of $655.2 million, which is well above the $101.4 million of EBITDA that the business generated last year. Of course, most of the debt is nonrecourse in nature. But all this means is that it is backed by the finance receivables that the business has on its books. If the debt goes unpaid, those finance receivables are used as collateral.

In order to improve operations, it is true that management is working on cost-cutting initiatives. In fact, management closed what it called its Phase 2 stores in the middle of January of this year. Phase 2 specifically refers to the second phase of its cost-cutting initiative. That's why the number of locations of the business in operation plummeted so much from the end of 2025 to the end of the third quarter of 2026. Management does expect there to be a financial benefit from the firm's consolidation activities that will be reflected in the final quarter of the year, before ultimately flowing through to the profit and loss statement in subsequent quarters as well. But until we have some idea of the long-term impact of this, plus we see stabilization from a sales standpoint, I believe that caution is warranted.

Takeaway

The way I see things, it is entirely possible that America's Car-Mart might turn around and see some impressive upside. But at the same time, there's a lot of risk here. The way that the economy is, combined with the weakness that the company has exhibited in recent years, is not confidence-inducing. So even though there might be some potential, my view is that maintaining it as a ‘sell’ makes the most sense.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Month Gold Broke: 5 Lessons From The 'March Madness' Selloff And The Rebound Opportunity
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA and Jonathan Flynn

Introduction: The Golden Illusion

For decades, investors have treated gold as the ultimate financial anchor, the potential "safe haven" that holds steady when the rest of the world is in flames. However, the behavior of gold’s price in March 2026 provided a brutal reality check that challenged this long-held assumption. In a move that caught many off guard, gold plummeted 12% to finish the month at US$4,608/oz.1

This wasn’t just a minor correction; it was the metal's weakest monthly performance since June 2013.2 While gold remains up for the year, the suddenness of the drop left many wondering how an asset designed to protect against volatility could suddenly become a primary source of it. The answer lies not in a sudden loss of faith in gold’s value, but in the cold, hard mechanics of global liquidity.

It Wasn’t the News—It Was the Exit Door

When markets panic, the first thing investors reach for isn’t necessarily the safest asset but the most liquid one. The March 2026 drawdown in gold’s price was a classic example of "deleveraging" taking precedence over long-term fundamentals.

The data paints a starker picture than the headlines suggest. Global gold exchange-traded products shed 84 tons (US$12bn) in March 2026, but the pain was geographically concentrated. North America led the exodus with a massive 87-ton outflow (US$14bn), while Europe lost 7 tons. In a fascinating "East-West" divide, Asia saw a welcome positive inflow of 10 tons (US$1.9bn), as regional investors viewed the carnage as a buying opportunity.3

Under the hood, the "Momentum factors" were the primary culprits. Commodity Trading Advisors (CTAs) were positioned very long heading into mid-March before sharply unwinding those positions as the price trend reversed.4 While COMEX managed money net longs dropped by US$2bn (19 tons), the underlying signal for bulls is that the net long bias remains solid.5 The big players were forced to the exit door for liquidity, but they haven't abandoned the room entirely.

The Central Bank Plot Twist

The real wildcard emerged from Ankara. The Central Bank of the Republic of Turkey, a stalwart gold purchaser since 2017, suddenly became a significant source of supply. Turkey’s central bank utilized approximately 50 tons of gold as collateral, predominantly via swaps, to manage intense domestic currency pressure.6

This wasn't a change in long-term strategy but a tactical liquidity-driven move. It aligns with a broader global trend; the US Federal Reserve recently suggested that central banks were increasing their sales of US Treasuries to buffer against higher energy price risks.7 In a world of tightening liquidity, even the most dedicated central bank holders of gold could use their gold reserves as a tactical chip when their national currency is under fire.

The Bond Market Shock and "The Dollar Factor"

To understand the anatomy of this fall in the price of gold, we must look at the opportunity cost of holding gold. According to the Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM), Momentum was the single largest contributor to the negative return in March. However, the model also highlighted a startling "12% cumulative negative residual", which was a portion of the move that traditional macro factors simply couldn't explain.8

What we can explain is the bond market shock. The 10-year Treasury Inflation-Protected Security (TIPS) yield experienced a sharp jump, driven by rising nominal yields.9 When yields rise this quickly, gold is left with nowhere to hide. Gold returns are historically sensitive to real rates; as the opportunity cost of holding a non-yielding asset skyrocketed alongside a strengthening US dollar, the speed of the inflation shock accelerated the price reversal beyond what the fundamentals predicted.

Why the Middle East Didn't Move the Needle

Standard market wisdom suggests that regional conflict should provide an automatic tailwind for gold. March debunked this, proving that geopolitical risk is often secondary to global liquidity needs.

Disruptions in parts of the Middle East failed to move the global needle. In Dubai, a "no flights, no tourists" trend significantly weighed on local demand for jewelry and small bars.10 This lack of physical traffic resulted in a local discount in gold prices compared to COMEX futures, highlighting a sharp disconnect between physical and paper markets. Trading volumes in Dubai increased, but the flow was insufficient to offset the massive deleveraging occurring in Western financial hubs.11

The ‘Green Shoots’ of Stabilization

Despite the historic sell-off, we believe the market is beginning to find its footing. These "green shoots" suggest the so-called "March Madness" may have been a transitory liquidity event:

  • The dollar’s ceiling: The US dollar is struggling to sustain gains and has failed to push beyond recent highs, reducing a primary pressure point.
  • Exchange-traded product reversal: Early April has seen positive Exchange-traded product inflows across regions, suggesting the mass exodus of March has paused.12
  • Policy rhetoric: Emerging macro signals suggest that further policy tightening in the US is likely to be more rhetorical than realized, providing a floor for gold.13
  • Constructive bias: Options markets show a constructive bias over a medium-term horizon, as seen in the 6-month risk reversals.14
  • Physical Support: Retail and physical demand are appearing as the price stabilizes above key technical levels.

History Shows These Selloffs Create Windows of Opportunity for Both Gold and Gold Miners

Where we believe things get interesting is in reviewing what has tended to happen, historically, after pullbacks in gold’s price performance. We recognize that past performance does not tell us exactly what the future will bring, but the historical record is still informative.

In the analysis that follows, we use the following:

  • SPDR Gold Shares (GLD): This is the most widely followed exchange-traded product designed to deliver the return, minus fees and expenses, of holding physical gold in a vault. It is the largest by assets under management as of this writing.
  • VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX): This is the most widely followed exchange-traded product meant to deliver the return, minus fees and expenses, of holding gold mining equities. Its underlying index is the MarketVector™ Global Gold Miners Index.

Figure 1a indicates:

  • We have defined 3 distinct types of ‘drawdown’ when looking at GLD’s performance, which are a 10% pullback, a 20% pullback, and then what we call a ‘Shock trough’ which has been defined by a 20% pullback coupled with macroeconomic stress.
  • The critical question, once these periods are defined, is what has the gold price, shown as GLD, and the return of gold mining equities, shown as GDX, been after these moves. In Figure 1a, we look at periods 6-month and 12-month after the pullback, and we show the range of returns, meaning we indicate the worst and the best return value for each period and note that the majority of observations was between those values. None of this guarantees what may transpire in the future, but it is good to have this type of historical context for analysis.

Figure 1a: Drawdowns as Opportunity: Historical Returns for GLD and GDX

Source: Morningstar, with data accessed through WisdomTree’s Fund Compare system, with data reviewed since GLD’s inception, 11/18/2004 to denote the different types of pullbacks. Returns are shown in net asset value (NAV) terms. Each band of returns represents the high value and low value observed for each type of period, meaning for all the periods where GLD had a 10% pullback, the GLD return 6 months after drawdown saw a range of results between +6% and +10%. Other cells in the table can be interpreted analogously. Past performance is not indicative of future returns. Standardized performance for GLD and GDX is shown in Figure 2b.

Figure 1b looks at similar data points, but instead of asking, ‘what was the average experience after a certain pullback?’, we define certain known ‘tough periods’ and we look at the returns of GLD and GDX during certain periods of time after those known troughs.

  • The pattern that we saw in Figure 1a seems to hold, in that there are the known ‘trough’ periods where GLD’s performance experiences a drawdown of some magnitude. From the trough, the GLD return 6 and 12 months looked positive, and GDX’s return over those same periods also looked positive, but in a greater magnitude.

Figure 1b: GLD and GDX After Major Drawdowns: Evidence from Key Market Events

Source: Morningstar, with data accessed through WisdomTree’s Fund Compare system, with data reviewed since GLD’s inception, 11/18/2004 to denote the specific market event drawdowns. Returns are shown in net asset value (NAV) terms. Past performance is not indicative of future returns. Standardized performance for GLD and GDX is shown in Figure 2b.

Introducing Capital Efficient Exposure to Gold

One of the historical issues that we have seen investors have with exposure to gold or gold miners regards how to add it to an overall allocation. Adding asset classes after trough periods is not easy to do. Holding a ‘gold position’ in a portfolio is open to a number of critiques, including how the metal has no earnings and no dividends, and the miners have had long periods of lackluster returns. WisdomTree has developed two distinct strategies that seek to mitigate these issues:

  • WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Gold Miners Strategy Fund (GDMN): This strategy is designed to provide a 90% exposure to the equities of gold miners, 90% exposure to gold futures, and 10% exposure to short-term U.S. treasury securities to serve as collateral for the futures. It thereby removes the need to think about whether an allocation is to gold or gold miners and creates an avenue to gain this exposure to both within one strategy.
  • WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Equity Strategy Fund (GDE): This strategy is designed to provide a 90% exposure to 500 large market capitalization U.S. equities, weighted by market cap, 90% exposure to gold futures, and 10% exposure to short-term U.S. treasury securities to serve as collateral for the futures. Instead of needing to draw down a U.S. equity position to make room for a gold allocation, one can make one allocation that has exposure to both.

Figure 2a shows the live performance history of GDE and GDMN and how they have compared to GLD and GDX, which were mentioned earlier. Due to limited live track records, we couldn’t include them in the Figure 1a and 1b analyses.

GDMN has meaningfully outpaced GDX and GLD across all measured horizons, reinforcing that this leveraged exposure to underlying gold prices has been beneficial. The divergence is most pronounced over the past year, where miners delivered substantially higher returns, but the pattern persists over longer periods as well. Even year-to-date, GDMN is leading, albeit by a smaller margin. This suggests that improving margins, operational leverage, and investor positioning are amplifying gains, positioning gold miners as a higher-beta expression of the broader gold trend. For people with bullish theses on both gold and gold miners should review the structure and potential of GDMN, in our opinion.

Figure 2a: Gold Miners Lead the Rally

Figure 2b: Standardized Returns

Sources: Morningstar, FactSet and WisdomTree, specifically data is from the PATH Fund Comparison Tool, accessed as of April 9, 2026, but showing returns for the period ended April 8, 2026 for Figure 2a and March 31, 2026 for 2b. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

It’s clear that GDMN has an implicit double exposure to gold, which can come with volatility, layering together both the returns of gold futures and the returns of gold miners. GDE, on the other hand, is more geared towards asset allocation. A broad-based U.S. equity exposure alongside a gold futures exposure could be more likely to see lower internal correlation of returns. A reasonable hypothesis may be that GDMN should tend toward higher relative volatility, whereas GDE should tend toward lower relative volatility. We look at 1-Year rolling volatility figures in Figure 3. It is interesting how GLD and GDE's rolling 1-Year volatility measures are similar over much of the available time series, even though GDE has implicit leverage and GLD does not. This may not always be the case, but it is speaking to the lower correlation between broad U.S. equities and the returns of gold futures.

Figure 3: Rolling 1-Year Volatility

Sources: Morningstar, FactSet and WisdomTree, specifically data is from the PATH Fund Comparison Tool, accessed as of April 9, 2026, but showing 1-Year rolling volatility of returns for the period ended March 31, 2026. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Conclusion: Liquidity Needs Can Break Gold Price Rallies—But They Also Create Historical Opportunities

March was not a failure of gold’s role, but a reminder of how markets function under stress. Liquidity, not fundamentals, dictated price. Yet history suggests these moments rarely mark the end of the story—they create the setup. As flows stabilize, real rates peak, and positioning resets, gold and gold equities often recover meaningfully. For investors, the takeaway is clear: the same forces that drove the sharp selloff may now be laying the foundation for the next leg higher.

Figure 4: Additional Information

Sources: Fund sponsor web pages, with the asset under management data as of April 8, 2026. Subject to change.

  2. Source: Barron’s. (2026, March 31). Gold finishes quarter positive despite March decline.

  3. Source: World Gold Council. (2026). Gold ETF holdings and flows (March 2026 data).

  4. Source: TD Securities (via FXStreet Insights). (2026, March 26). Gold: CTA selling risk and structural cracks.

  5. Source: World Gold Council. (2026). COMEX net long positioning in gold futures.

  6. Source: Dhawan, S. (2026, April 8). Have central banks started selling gold? Financial Express.

  7. Sources: Financial Times. (2026, March 31). Foreign central banks cut U.S. Treasury holdings amid rising oil prices; Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System. (2026). Factors affecting reserve balances: H.4.1 release.

  8. Source: World Gold Council. (2026). Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM).

  9. Source: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (US). (2026). 10-year Treasury inflation-indexed securities (TIPS) yield.

  10. Source: Financial Times. (2026, March 4). Gold and silver flows disrupted as Iran war grounds flights.

  11. Source: Xie, Y., & Soni, P. (2026, March 6). Gold stuck in Dubai is being sold at a discount as war widens. Bloomberg.

  14. Source: City Index. (2026). Gold price outlook: Are options traders front-running a breakout?

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal.

GDMN: The Fund is actively managed and invests in U.S.-listed gold futures and global equity securities issued by companies that derive at least 50% of their revenue from the gold mining business (“Gold Miners”). The Fund’s use of U.S.-listed gold futures contracts will give rise to leverage, magnifying gains and losses and causing the Fund to be more volatile than if it had not been leveraged. Moreover, the price movements in gold and gold futures contracts may fluctuate quickly and dramatically, and have a historically low correlation with the returns of the stock and bond markets. By investing in the equity securities of Gold Miners, the Fund may be susceptible to financial, economic, political, or market events that impact the gold mining sub-industry, including commodity prices and the success of exploration projects. The Fund may invest a significant portion of its assets in the securities of companies of a single country or region, including emerging markets, and thus, the Fund is more likely to be impacted by events and political, economic, or regulatory conditions affecting that country or region, or emerging markets generally. While the Fund is actively managed, the Fund’s investment process is heavily dependent on quantitative models and the models may not perform as intended.

GDE: The Fund is actively managed and invests in U.S. listed gold futures and U.S. equity securities. The Fund’s use of U.S. listed gold futures contracts will give rise to leverage, magnifying gains and losses and causing the Fund to be more volatile than if it had not been leveraged. Moreover, the price movements in gold and gold futures contracts may fluctuate quickly and dramatically, and have a historically low correlation with the returns of the stock and bond markets. While the Fund is actively managed, the Fund's investment process is heavily dependent on quantitative models and the models may not perform as intended.

Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006.
