data.title: Retail Traders Retreat Amid Iran Conflict
data.description:
By Phil Mackintosh, Nasdaq Chief Economist

Since late 2024, retail traders have become even more active than they were in the years following the pandemic. At the end of last year, our retail data suggests retail trading reached $70 billion a day as markets rotated out of crypto and software stocks and into value stocks.

But in the past few weeks, as the conflict in Iran started, data shows retail trading has fallen significantly. Interestingly, so did the price of gold.

Gross trading falls as conflict continues

Amid the conflict, exchange-traded fund (ETF) activity has increased, as ETFs are a natural way to play a macro event. However, retail saw reduced ETF activity (yellow line), which fell by around $5 billion per day.

Single-stock retail trading tumbled even more - below $35 billion a day. After a ceasefire was put in place on April 8, stock flows rebounded above $40 billion a day.

Chart 1: Gross daily retail trading activity in stocks and ETFs over time

Notably, retail remained net buyers of ETFs throughout the first quarter of 2026. Overall, retail bought more than $19 billion in ETFs in March and bought over $70 billion in ETFs during the first quarter.

However, data shows that retail traders were sellers of single stocks every single day in March. As a result, March saw net selling of over $15 billion - a record for the periods we have data for.

Chart 2: Net monthly retail trading activity in stocks and ETFs over time

Single-stock sector flows show a change in leadership

Looking at single-stock flows by company each month, we see a change in leadership over the years.

Initially, Tesla (TSLA) was one of the most popular retail stocks, although between 2020 and 2023, Tesla buying and selling tended to follow the direction of the market (buying in up markets, green line, and vice versa).

In the past two years, NVIDIA (NVDA) has taken over as the most popular and most heavily bought stock.

Interestingly, as the conflict continued, net flows across all sectors turned negative, and buying and selling in NVIDIA fell to roughly balanced.

Chart 3: Single-stock sector flows by month over time

First significant dip in U.S. retail trading in years

Although retail trading has grown significantly since 2019, and net trading has fluctuated often with the direction of the market, this represents the first significant reduction in gross retail trading activity since 2021 - retail didn’t even have this strong of an immediate reaction to the war in Ukraine.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Definium: 'Strong Buy' Maintained On Several Anticipated 2026 Milestones
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Definium Therapeutics, Inc. (DFTX) it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Mind Medicine: Two Pathways Created From One Pipeline Clinical Candidate." In that article, I mentioned two specific programs that I went over, which were the use of MM-120 [now known as DT120] for the treatment of patients with generalized anxiety disorder [GAD] and major depressive disorder [MDD].

Before going further, it is important to state that at the beginning of this year, the company had decided to change its name from Mind Medicine over to be that of Definium Therapeutics. The reason for the name change was because of its focus on treating psychiatric disorders, plus its focus on the anticipation of three data readouts in 2026. Speaking of which, the last time I spoke about this company, I had given it a Strong Buy rating.

With everything it has accomplished thus far, I believe that it is important to maintain this rating. Why is that? Well, that's because of the three expected data readouts that are rapidly approaching. With respect to DT120 ODT [lysergide tartrate] for the treatment of patients with GAD, it is running the ongoing phase 3 Voyage and Panorama studies. Top-line data from each of these studies are anticipated to be released in Q3 and the 2nd half of 2026, respectively. In terms of DT120 ODT for the treatment of patients with MDD, this program is in the process of running the phase 3 Emerge and Ascend studies. It's important to highlight that topline data from one of these two studies, Emerge, is what is set to be released in early Q2 of 2026. In terms of the other late-stage trial, Ascend, this is only expected to dose the first patient in early Q2 of 2026.

Another positive to consider with respect to this rating is that the company is in the process of developing R(-)-MDMA DT402 for the treatment of adult patients with autism spectrum disorder [ASD] in an ongoing phase 2a study that was initiated in Q4 of 2025. I will be going over this program below, as I did not touch on it last time around, and it offers additional potential for Definium going forward.

The final reason for my Strong Buy rating is because of what just transpired the other day for psychedelic companies, which is that President Donald Trump and its administration signaled plans to have an executive order to help increase research into ibogaine.

With three data readouts expected this year from the development of DT120 ODT for the treatment of patients with GAD/MDD, plus the ability to evaluate the use of R(-)-MDMA DT402 for the treatment of patients with ASD, I believe that this stock should be kept at a Strong Buy rating. Should the data readouts go bad, depending upon the data released, I will have to reevaluate again at such time.

Autism Spectrum Disorder Allows Company To Branch Out With DT402

As I mentioned above, Definium Therapeutics is doing very well with its other late-stage trial programs. It is expected to release data from its Emerge study of DT120 ODT targeting patients with MDD in late Q2 of 2026. From there, I mentioned that other data readouts are set to follow from the Voyage and Panorama studies for the treatment of patients with GAD thereafter. This time around, I believe it is important to focus on the development of another candidate from its pipeline, which is that of DT402 as an R(-)-MDMA for the treatment of patients with autism spectrum disorder [ASD]. It is in good shape with respect to this program because it already had obtained a positive outcome of using this drug candidate in a prior phase 1 healthy volunteer study. Even better, as mentioned beforehand, it already initiated a phase 2a study using DT402 to treat these patients.

Before going over the entire clinical program in detail, plus any catalysts to come about from this program, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Autism spectrum disorder [ASD] is characterized as a brain development issue disorder where a person has an issue with how they view other people and developmental problems. Another thing to note is that this is like a broad disorder, where it used to be separated into three other categories, such as:

  • Asperger syndrome
  • Pervasive development problems
  • Autism.

The term "spectrum" denotes that a person has a wide range of issues that they have to deal with. There are social issues and behavior issues in place. The company is going to use DT402 specifically to tackle the social and communication issues that are in place in these ASD patients. In addition, while this disorder starts off in children, eventually they become adults who have it. Thus, the focus of this company in the ongoing phase 2a study is only to target adults with ASD as part of its clinical evaluation.

The global autism spectrum disorder treatment market is projected to reach $18.68 billion by 2034. This is a very large market opportunity, but how can DT402 possibly help? The reason why is because at the present time there are no pharmacotherapies approved for the treatment of core symptoms of ASD [Definium Therapeutics Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #26]. The thing is that MDMA might be practical, but it has safety issues. This is where an (R)-MDMA might be able to do a better job at helping patients. The notion is to still offer the therapeutic effect of retaining the psychoactive drug effect for a person, but with an improved safety profile. The point of this drug is not to cure ASD, but instead to help with increasing social activity and being less anxious around people. An MDMA drug involves targeting a combination of components of the amygdala [a gray matter space in the brain that controls emotions], such as having an effect on serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine, alongside increasing oxytocin levels.

In order to determine if DT402 is going to be effective in treating communication and social issues of adult patients with ASD, it is running an ongoing phase 2a trial. This specific mid-stage study is expected to recruit up to a total of 20 adults with the disorder, who are to be given a single dose of 200 mg of DT402. The primary efficacy endpoint to be evaluated is that of change from baseline in the 11-point Numerical Rating Scale [NRS] scores. This score looks at disease severity ranging from "0" to being "no effect" all the way to "10" of being severe effects in place. This score looks at both the positive and negative symptoms of ASD. Multiple functional biomarkers are to be evaluated from this study as well.

Speaking of which, the reason to bring up this program is because this is another catalyst for investors to focus on during this year. In 2026, Definium has a plan to release initial data from this phase 2a study using DT402 for the treatment of patients with ASD. One thing for sure is thus far that this drug candidate has been shown to be safe. That's because in order for the company to initiate this phase 2a study, it had to observe an excellent safety profile from its phase 1 healthy volunteer study that was completed. Along with pharmacodynamic [PD] and pharmacokinetic [PK] profiles that allowed it to move forward in clinical testing.

With all of the programs noted above, that is why I have given the rating I have. There was another positive that warrants this rating as well, which is that on Thursday CBS News reported that President Donald Trump signaled plans to issue an executive disorder that would allow the potential to increase the research happening around ibogaine [a traditional psychedelic plant native to Africa]. Besides Definium being one of the many psychedelic beneficiaries of this move, other stocks that traded higher in tandem were Compass Pathways (CMPS), Atai Life Sciences (ATAI), GH Research (GHRS) and Cybin (HELP). This is bullish, and the hope is that many of these psychedelic companies can benefit. However, the report appears to note the need to look at developing ibogaine specifically for brain issues for, veterans, along with post-traumatic stress disorder [PTSD].

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC filing, Definium Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and investments of $411.6 million as of December 31, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand is because back on October 29, 2025, it entered into an underwriting agreement in connection with an underwritten public offering of common stock. This October 2025 offering involved Jefferies LLC, Leerink Partners LLC, and Evercore Group L.L.C. as well. It was able to raise a total of $242.8 million after expenses. This was thanks to it selling a total of 18,375,000 shares of its common stock at a price of $12.25 per share. Along with the fact that it granted the underwriters an option to purchase up to an additional 2,756,250 shares of common stock at the very same price. Of which, the underwriters exercised their option in full.

The truth is that this company has plenty of cash runway. Why is that? That's because it believes that its cash on hand is enough to fund its operations into 2028. Barring some unforeseen cash raise to come about [if it releases positive data causing the stock price to trade higher], then it is in good shape. Especially since it also has another financial tool at its disposal, which is a sales agreement established back on June 28, 2024, with Leerink Partners LLC. The reason for this was so that the company could from time to time offer and sell up to $150 million worth of common shares. The good thing is that this company has not yet tapped into this sales agreement. As of December 31, 2025, no shares have been sold under it.

Risks To Business

There are several risks to consider before investing in Definium Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the first expected data readout. This relates to the release of topline data from the phase 3 Emerge study using DT120 ODT for the treatment of patients with MDD, expected in late Q2 of 2026. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that the primary endpoint of change from baseline in the Montgomery-Asberg Depression Rating Scale [MADRS] total score at week 6 will be met in a statistically significant manner. In addition, this is only the first phase 3 trial for this program. The second phase 3 study, to be known as Ascend, is not expected to have the first patient dosed until early Q2 of 2026. The risk here is that even if one study ends up being successful, there is no guarantee that a similar or superior outcome will be achieved by the other.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the other program that this company is working on, which is the evaluation of DT120 ODT for the treatment of patients with GAD in the ongoing phase 3 Voyage and Panorama studies. The company expects to release data readouts from both of these studies in early Q3 of 2026 and the 2nd half of 2026, respectively. The company was able to initiate this late-stage trial program because it achieved durable, clinically meaningful, statistically significant data when it came to both the HAM-A and MADRS scales evaluating DT120 ODT over a 12-week period. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the statistical significance achieved in prior testing will yet again be replicated in either of these two ongoing phase 3 studies.

The third and final risk to consider would be the program I went over above, which is the use of another MDMA, or R(-)-MDMA DT402, for the treatment of patients with ASD in the ongoing phase 2a study. The company was able to boast an excellent safety profile along with substantial PK/PD data in a phase 1 healthy volunteer study that it allowed to move on to the phase 2a study. The risk here is that, first and foremost, there is no guarantee that there won't be any safety issues observed in the ongoing study. Secondly, the second study is intended to provide more detail into the use of this drug with respect to functional biomarkers. Even though the safety profile might remain intact, there is no guarantee that the functional biomarker data is going to end up being positive upon the data being released in 2026. Nor that such clinical data is going to be enough to allow the company to move this program forward into late-stage clinical studies. If this candidate fails to deliver a positive outcome in the phase 2a study, then this means the company will have to rely on the development of DT120 ODT for the treatment of patients with MDD and GAD.

Conclusion

Definium Therapeutics is in good shape, as prior testing allowed it to achieve statistical significance in using DT120 ODT for the treatment of patients with GAD. This was both in terms of the HAM-A and MADRS scales over a 12-week period. There is huge potential for this company as it approaches a catalyst-rich period in 2026. There are not only three phase 3 studies that have a data readout relating to DT120 ODT for both MDD and GAD, but there is also the other opportunity with DT402. That is, the use of this other drug candidate to treat the social and communication issues for patients with ASD.

With the progress made to date in advancing several phase 3 studies with DT120 ODT since I last wrote about this company, plus the potential to expand to its other drug candidate DT402, I believe that it is important to maintain a Strong Buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Is ChargePoint Holdings Poised For A Comeback? (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Overview

My last coverage on ChargePoint Holdings, Inc. (CHPT) stock was on October 27, 2024. Considering the top-line and cash burn trend, I had opined that CHPT stock is a “Sell.” Since that coverage, the stock has witnessed a significant correction of 76%.

As I reinitiate coverage on ChargePoint, I am upgrading the stock from “Sell” to “Buy.” In my view, the steep correction has translated into an attractive buying opportunity. While some challenges remain, the stock seems to have found a bottom.

As a matter of fact, CHPT stock has rallied by almost 15% in the last month. Further, according to regulatory filings, the company’s President & CEO, Richard Wilmer, purchased 46,847 shares of common stock on April 13, 2026.

Of course, insider buying is not the catalyst for a rating upgrade. This thesis discusses the factors that point to a potential reversal in trend for CHPT stock over the next 12 to 24 months.

Impact Of Iran Conflict On Electric Vehicles

While efforts continue towards de-escalation in the Middle East, there is likely to be a long-lasting impact of the war on consumer decisions. According to SNE Research, the conflict is likely to trigger a “sharp rebound” in the global EV market. Based on the analysis:

Global demand is expected to accelerate by 0.5 years this year compared to previous forecasts, by 1 year in 2027, and by more than 2 years from 2028 onwards.

As the chart below shows, the impact is likely to be sustained beyond the decade.

According to Ajay Bhatia, chief executive of Mobile.de (Germany’s biggest online car marketplace), there has been a 50% increase in electric car inquiries in March compared with February. This is a clear indication of the impact of rising fuel prices. Even after de-escalation, consumers will likely discount this risk while making a purchase decision.

A similar trend is observed in the used car EV market in Europe. Consumers are reacting to higher fuel prices and potentially gravitating towards EVs and hybrids.

It’s worth noting that eight million electric cars in the European Union will be saving 46 million barrels of oil in 2025. This implies “€2.9 billion in avoided oil import costs.” With the impact visible, the shift towards EVs is likely to get a push from policymakers considering the fuel price uncertainty.

Overall, there is a clear indication of potential acceleration in EV sales in the coming years. This would imply growing demand for EV charging infrastructure in the United States and Europe. Even amidst intense competition, ChargePoint seems positioned to benefit.

To put things into perspective, it’s estimated that Europe needs to “install 500,000 public charging piles per year by 2030 and 1 million per year thereafter.” Given the demand scenario, I don’t see competition as a headwind. Multiple players can grow at a healthy pace beyond the decade.

Similarly, for the United States, more than 42.2 million charging ports will be needed by 2035 to support 78.5 million EVs. This includes Level 2 chargers as well as DC fast chargers.

Positives From Q4 2026 Results

What’s discussed thus far is an industry perspective. This section will discuss the company-specific positives that support the rating upgrade.

When I discussed ChargePoint in October 2024, a key concern was top-line degrowth at a time when some peers were delivering growth. For FY26, ChargePoint reported revenue of $411 million as compared to $417 million in FY25. Revenue has therefore been flat.

However, for Q4 2026, ChargePoint reported 7.3% year-on-year growth in revenue to $109.3 million. ChargePoint believes that the company is likely to return to accelerated growth later this year and into FY28. This view is backed by the point that the company is ramping up new product volume in the coming quarters.

Another major concern that I discussed in my earlier thesis was cash burn. I believe that there are two important factors that are likely to translate into margin improvement and lower cash burn.

First, ChargePoint is pursuing significant cost cutting. For Q4 2026, loss from operations was marginally lower on a year-on-year basis, and I expect the trend to sustain. As an example, R&D expense as a percentage of revenue was 43.6% in FY24 and has declined to 33.9% in FY26.

Second, ChargePoint announced its “Flex” product line last year. The company is already ramping up single-port AC product for both home and fleet. At the same time, ChargePoint is launching its next-gen DC product with ramp-up scheduled in the second half of FY27. The key point to note is that both these products have a better margin profile. Therefore, there is likely to be a positive impact on EBITDA margin in FY27 and FY28.

From a margin perspective, it’s worth noting that FY26 revenue declined by 1.4% on a year-on-year basis. However, for the same period, subscription revenue increased by 12.5%. As the number of charging ports increases, subscription revenue should continue to swell.

Further, as ChargePoint continues to introduce new tools for improved EV charging management, it’s likely that subscription revenue growth will accelerate. As a matter of fact, the company’s subscription revenue as a percentage of total revenue has swelled from 23.8% in FY24 to 39.5% in FY26.

It’s worth adding here that the cost of network charging systems as a percentage of revenue was 48.6% in FY26. Cost of subscription revenue as a percentage of revenue was only 15%. This underscores my view on sustained margin expansion as subscription revenue swells.

ChargePoint is also positive on the implementation of AI and its impact on key margins. According to the company, AI is now "starting to show tangible results” in terms of keeping the cost constant “while growing top line and expanding our product portfolio.”

Coming back to top-line growth catalysts, ChargePoint reported 77% revenue from North America and 23% from Europe. However, for Q4 2026, Europe delivered “robust double-digit growth, driven by regulations and new incentives.” ChargePoint expects this trend to continue in FY27. It’s likely that Europe’s contribution to total revenue will increase and support overall growth.

From a liquidity perspective, ChargePoint ended FY26 with a cash buffer of $142 million. The company believes that the cash, coupled with cash from sales, will support working capital requirements for the next 12 months. Therefore, there seems to be no immediate need for capital infusion through dilution of equity.

Another positive from a balance sheet perspective is that ChargePoint ended FY26 with total debt of $260.9 million. Total debt was $36.2 million lower on a year-on-year basis. With the possibility of growth acceleration and margin improvement, it’s likely that ChargePoint will witness significant improvement in credit metrics in the next 24 months.

Risk Factors To The Thesis

For FY26, ChargePoint reported a non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA loss of $82.7 million. Losses have narrowed from $116.5 million in FY25. However, it’s unlikely that ChargePoint will achieve adjusted EBITDA break-even in FY27. If cash burn sustains, there is a case for equity dilution or leveraging in FY28. Having said that, cost-cutting coupled with better margin products is likely to ensure continued narrowing of EBITDA-level losses.

As of FY26, ChargePoint reported inventory of $214.9 million. On a year-on-year basis, inventory has increased marginally. One reason for inventory remaining high is the introduction of the company’s next-generation AC and DC networked models. The new products might also increase the risk of existing products becoming obsolete, translating into inventory write-downs. The higher level of inventory implies an elevated requirement for working capital and impacts gross margin. The company, however, believes that there will be a “gradual reduction” in inventory through FY27. This is likely to have a positive impact on cash flows.

From the perspective of competition, I already elaborated on the industry potential. Even as numbers of players in the industry increase, there is headroom for growth. Additionally, ChargePoint is focused on next-generation products and software. This is likely to ensure that the company maintains or increases its market share.

Valuation Perspective

From a valuation perspective, ChargePoint is still reporting EBITDA-level losses. It’s therefore not possible to consider a peer analysis based on the EV/EBITDA valuation metric.

However, there are other ratios that point to ChargePoint trading at an attractive valuation. As the table below indicates, ChargePoint is trading at a discount to peer valuation averages on multiple metrics.

It’s also worth noting that ChargePoint holds a cash buffer of $142 million as of FY26. In comparison, a market valuation of $152 million looks attractive. Of course, the reasons for depressed valuations include cash burn and growth concerns. However, ChargePoint is likely to address these headwinds in the next 12 to 24 months. I will therefore not be surprised if there is a meaningful rally from current levels.

Concluding Views

ChargePoint stock has eroded shareholder wealth in the past. However, with the management taking corrective measures to curb costs and with innovation-driven products, the outlook seems optimistic.

The company has already registered top-line growth in Q4. With product ramp-up, it’s likely that growth will accelerate, coupled with EBITDA margin expansion. This is likely to result in CHPT stock trending higher from deeply oversold levels.

From an industry perspective, the Middle East war and rising fuel prices have the potential to accelerate consumer preference towards electric vehicles. This will benefit ChargePoint, which has a strong presence in North America and a growing presence in Europe.

data.title: Liquidity Drain Underway, Will It Even Matter?
data.description:
Liquidity is draining from the market, as expected; whether risk assets feel it or not is the only question that remains. Historically, the past four years have shown that the second half of April tends to be flat to down. So far, that is proving to be wrong.

As of April 15, the Treasury General Account has risen to around $925 billion, up $697 billion from April 8. The TGA is likely to top out around $1 trillion and should result in reserve balances held at the Fed in the $2.8 to $2.9 trillion range.

As of Wednesday, April 15, reserve balances at the Fed fell to around $2.98 trillion and are likely to decline further toward the level last seen in October and November. This pushed reserves down to a very low level, one that could be deemed no longer sufficient to support the overnight funding market, given the spikes in usage of the standing repo facility and the widening funding spreads. This week has seen the secured overnight financing rate climb back over the effective federal funds rate, with the spread widening to around 3 bps.

This could put the markets at the start of a regime shift in liquidity, with reserves falling back to the lower end of the ample range, and this was the primary reason the Fed began its reserve management program by buying Treasury bills. However, this follows a period in which liquidity was rising, as evidenced by the TGA's drawdown and the rise in reserve balances.

Historically, when we look at the impact of reserves following an increase in the TGA, it has been negative for risk assets around the April tax day. Whether that pattern continues to hold is up for debate, as nobody can tell the future. But we do know that when the Treasury General Account rises, the Fed's reserves fall, which can lead to tighter overnight funding conditions. So the idea that liquidity is being removed from the market is sound and supported by the recent overnight market reactions.

Additionally, we know that around tax season, we tend to see risk assets struggle. Of course, it is not easy to pick the exact moment when this should or shouldn't start. But it would suggest that, historically, the second half of April tends to be more challenging, likely due to changes in liquidity levels.

Date Range

Approximate SPX Move

Peak to Trough (Rounded)
April 13-May 4, 2022 -9%
April 11–April 26, 2023 -1.5ƒ%
April 10–April 24, 2024 -5%
April 14–April 21, 2025 -6.5%

At least as of April 17, 2026, the theory seems not to be working. Consider that since April 14, 2026, the S&P 500 (SP500) has risen by roughly 2.5%, which would make this year an outlier in the analysis, at least based on the historical trends of the past four years.

Trying to predict the future is not an easy task, but understanding how things tend to flow based on historical trends can certainly help shift the odds somewhat.

So while there is no doubt that TGA has risen, as expected, and reserve balances have fallen and will likely have some further to fall. The question remains open as to whether risk assets will feel the pinch once again as they have in previous years.

data.title: 2 ETF Smart Leverage Portfolio Could Beat The S&P 500 By 200% Over 25 Years
data.description:
What if I told you that you can beat the S&P 500 (SPY) by 200% over the next 25 years? That would mean roughly tripling your ending portfolio balance in 2050 compared to if it were passively invested in the US large-cap equity index — e.g., end up with $1.5 million instead of $500,000. And what if I told you that this can be done rather simply via an investment in only two ETFs at most, and with less risk, as measured by worst declines from all-time highs and worst daily returns?

Ok, to be candid, I cannot promise that we can all achieve results like this. But if any of us wants to give it a try, I have an idea for maximizing the chances of success.

No, I don't have strong opinions or insights about how Bitcoin (GBTC)(BITO), gold (GLD) and silver (SLV), or the semiconductor space (SOXX) will fare in the next few years or decades. I also don't know which asset class or security will see its price skyrocket next. Instead, producing generational wealth well above market pace is likely best approached with at least some multi-asset diversification and, probably more importantly, systematically (and not instinctively) knowing when to use leverage and when to de-risk the portfolio.

Doing the math

Before diving into the details, let's do some quick math to figure out how much annual outperformance vs. the equities benchmark one would need to produce in order to beat the S&P 500 by a cumulative 200% over 25 years. The answer is probably a bit less than many of us might imagine — a byproduct of the power of compounding over the long haul.

Since 1980, dividends included, the S&P 500 has gained 11.5% per year, which is quite impressive. The rise in stock prices has been fueled by a bullish cocktail of falling interest rates, rising valuations, globalization, and the tech productivity boom, among a few other factors. If such gains were replicated over the next two and a half decades (more on this at the end of this article), an initial investment of $100,000 today would grow about 15-fold to $1.5 million by the end of 2050. To end up with a final balance that is 200% higher, i.e., $4.5 million, the necessary annual returns would need to be in the order of 16.5% per year.

While I wouldn't argue that beating the S&P 500 consistently by five percentage points per year is easy, it "feels" doable, assuming a sensible investment strategy that blends aggressiveness and downside risk mitigation.

Busting a myth: leverage increases risk

I want to start our conversation by being a contrarian and challenging the perception that leverage necessarily increases portfolio risk. In some cases, as discussed next, leverage can disproportionally boost upside potential relative to the additional volatility and losses that it may produce, if any.

The secret here is to know when to deploy leverage and when to dial down risk exposure in a systematic way. And here, again, I will be a contrarian: in my view, investors should generally increase leverage when asset prices are near peak levels; and delever (possibly exiting a long position altogether) when asset prices are retreating. You read that right: buy high, sell low — a strategy whose appeal is supported by careful analysis of the historical data, as presented below.

Since 1980, the S&P 500 has produced an annualized volatility of 17.9%. However, this number merely represents an average of good times and bad times in the equities market and deserves to be further dissected.

Volatility tends to be substantially higher when stocks enter correction territory, defined for our purposes today as a 10%-plus pullback from the highs of the prior six months. Also worth noting, volatility tends to increase meaningfully when asset prices go down and decrease when prices go up.

What if I were to split the performance of the S&P 500 between (1) when the index is trading within 10% of its six-month highs (a.k.a., bull periods), and (2) when it dips below such levels (a.k.a., bear periods)? In this case, historical volatility since 1980 would be as follows:

  • During bull periods (81% of the time): 13.8% annualized volatility.
  • During bear periods (19% of the time): 29.5% annualized volatility.

Leverage, which can boost the performance of a portfolio substantially, is most dangerous when deployed during times of high volatility because it amplifies the losses that are inherent in high-volatility periods and it produces the most volatility drag, hence even more losses, which readers can learn more about from sources like the SEC. So, when I look at the volatility numbers above, broken down across bull and bear periods, the first logical thought that crosses my mind is that:

I should feel much more comfortable leveraging my exposure to the S&P 500 when the index is trading within 10% of the highs (which has happened roughly in four out of five trading days, on average), and not the opposite.

Of course, risk is not necessarily the same as volatility. An arguably better definition of risk is the probability of a sizable loss from peak levels that is generally difficult to recover. In this regard, the idea of leveraging the S&P 500 only near highs makes even more sense.

The chart below shows how being divested when the S&P 500 was undergoing a correction would have prevented an investor from partaking in painful bear-market retreats. Those include the 1980s Volcker shock period, the early 2000s dot-com crash, the 2008 Great Financial Crisis, and even Black Monday in 1987 (i.e., all the periods not highlighted in light blue below).

So far, the key takeaway is that, as evidenced by nearly 50 years of historical performance, owning the S&P 500 on leverage makes quite a bit more sense when the index is trading near highs. Generational wealth, therefore, is likely easier to produce when an investor aggressively rides the "beta tailwinds" of market tranquility.

A simple trading strategy

Next, I will propose a minimally diversified portfolio allocation strategy that takes advantage of what I discussed above, properly backtested over the last few decades. The trading idea would follow this straightforward set of rules:

  • During bull periods (i.e., when the S&P 500 is not in a correction), which I expect to be most of the time, split the portfolio 50/50 between longer-duration treasuries and a 3x leveraged S&P 500 position. The former can be achieved today with a simple investment in the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), while the latter would require holding a fund like the ProShares UltraPro S&P500 ETF (UPRO) or the Direxion Daily S&P 500 Bull 3X ETF (SPXL). Effectively, this would be a 2x leveraged position between US stocks and government bonds, with the notional being allocated 150% to equities and 50% to treasuries.
  • During bear periods (i.e., when the S&P 500 is in a correction), exit the leveraged S&P 500 position and allocate 100% to TLT.

Below is a chart (in log scale) alongside a brief table that shows how this simple strategy would have fared since 1986 (as far back as I could go to retrieve daily asset prices for both stocks and bonds). Notice that the leveraged portfolio strategy would have outperformed the S&P 500 across the board, other than on annualized volatility: much better absolute returns, better volatility-adjusted returns, tamer maximum drawdown, and much better worst day ever.

It is worth highlighting the 17.1% annual return above. The 6.1 percentage-point advantage over SPY would have been more than enough to beat the equities index by 200% over 25 years, as the title of this article proposes.

Of course, implementing such a trading strategy would require being comfortable holding a leveraged S&P 500 position most of the time, and daily monitoring of the portfolio over many, many years. This is why I think that producing significant alpha, very loosely defined here as absolute returns above the S&P 500, is likely more a function of diligent execution than the sophistication of the investment strategy.

On the other hand, what the smart leverage approach would likely not require is ownership of any more than two highly liquid ETFs during bull periods in equities and a single ETF during bear periods, which underscores the simplicity of the methodology, and without too many trades. Regarding the latter, my backtest suggests that the portfolio allocation would only need to shift between the two ETFs 2.2% of the trading days, or about once every two months, on average.

Looking forward

All the above speak to the strategy applied to the performance of stocks and bonds since the 1980s, which is backward-looking. There is no guarantee, for example, that US stocks will continue to return 11.5% per year indefinitely.

However, I can roughly estimate what the proposed smart leverage strategy might produce in gains between now and 2050. I can start with the treasury piece of the portfolio, which is easier to assess.

Historically, bonds and bond funds have produced gains over their average maturity periods that roughly equal the yield on that particular fixed income instrument or basket of instruments when it was purchased. That is: buying TLT today is likely to result in annual gains of nearly 5.0% through the next 20-plus years, which is the average yield to maturity of the fund.

A quick back-of-the-envelope calculation suggests that, if I allocate 50% of my portfolio to TLT 81% of the time (bull periods) and 100% to the ETF 19% of the time (bear periods), my annual expected return should average about 3.0% in the long run.

For the equity piece, I will anticlimactically use Capital Asset Pricing Model as my north star — not a perfect approach, but one that I will call "good enough" for now. If the 10-year Treasury yield is currently 4.25% and Kroll has set its estimate of the equity risk premium at 5.0%, I might expect SPY to produce 9.25% in gains per year going forward, which I consider reasonable and intuitive.

Next, the data suggests that leveraging the S&P 500 by 1.5x during bull periods and selling the position during bear periods (no allocation to bonds, in this case) has historically increased the annualized return of SPY by an average of 1.2x while maintaining annual volatility roughly the same and cutting maximum drawdown by about three-fourths (see chart below, log scale).

Therefore, I can multiply 1.2 times 9.25% for the equity piece, then add the 3.0% expected return in bonds to estimate that the smart leverage strategy that I proposed earlier might realistically produce 14.0% in annualized returns over a 25-year period, or nearly five percentage points of additional return relative to my expectations of SPY.

At this pace, investors might be able to beat the S&P 500 by roughly 200% cumulatively by 2050 — and improve their chances of retiring with a significantly larger nest egg.

data.title: UnitedHealth Group: Poised To Challenge The $360 Resistance
data.description:
UNH stock: substantial margin recovery potential

I last covered UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH, UNH:CA) on Feb 5, 2026. That article rated the stock as Buy after reviewing its FQ4 2025 earnings report (ER). Since then, there have been a few notable developments around the healthcare sector as a whole and also specific to the UNH stock. The remainder of this article will detail my thoughts on 3 of them: the company’s upcoming FQ1 2026 ER on April 21st, the changes in Medicare Advantage rate, and also the stock’s latest technical trading patterns. These developments have led me to see large potential for the stock price to break out the $360 resistance level in the near term and to reiterate my buy rating.

Let me start with the upcoming ER. As seen in the chart below, UNH is scheduled to release its FQ1 2026 ER on April 21. The latest earnings revision reveals a massive profitability inflection point this quarter. To wit, in the previous quarter (with Earnings announced on January 27, 2026), the company continued to suffer a compressed net margin (of around 1.70% only on a normalized basis). The upcoming projections signal a radical recovery. To wit, for the upcoming quarter, the EPS Normalized Estimate is set to surge to $6.62 on total revenue of $109.66B. These projection imply a net margin of 5.53% assuming the same share count as the previous quarter.

Next, I will explain why this recovery is very likely.

UNH stock’s profitability metrics

To better contextualize further discussions, the chart below shows how UnitedHealth’s current profitability compares against peers and its historical norms. I see a clear and significant divergence between both by vertical and horizontal comparisons. As seen, the company’s current margins have been substantially below the sector median (with the exception of its Net Income Margin, NIM) and also its historical averages. Its TTM NIM is only 2.69%, which, only about half of its own five-year average of 5.19%.

Against this background, you can see why the FQ1 implied NIM caught my attention. The implied NIM of 5.53% is not only a massive expansion from its TTM and previous quarter’s margins but also exceeds the long-term average of 5.19%. I expect such an expansion, if confirmed in the upcoming ER, to trigger a sizable revelation of the shares in the upward direction.

UNH stock: tailwinds not fully priced in yet

And I do see plenty of catalysts that could help materialize such an expansion and even further boost its margins in the years to come. The company has been strategically repricing its policies and optimizing its membership lately. In terms of operation, the company has also been working on the use of AI technologies to drive efficiency. I anticipate seeing tangible benefits from efforts in the upcoming ER. Furthermore, the recent rate change announced by the Medicare Advantage program (see the details quoted below) should also provide a nice boost to its EPS in the next few years.

Seeking Alpha news: The Trump administration will increase average Medicare Advantage payments by 2.48%, or more than $13 billion, in 2027, according to Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). The Trump administration in January proposed a payment rate hike of 0.09%, which pummeled shares of insurers that run those plans. The closely watched government payment rate determines how much insurers can charge for monthly premiums and plan benefits they offer and, ultimately, their profits.

Yet, these catalysts are not fully priced in in my assessment. With all the positives just mentioned, UnitedHealth’s current valuation profile presents large odds for further upside in my view. To wit, the stock carries a forward non-GAAP P/E of 17.7 only. This is about 13% below its five-year average of 20.3. By a horizontal comparison, this is on par with the sector median (with a difference of less than 1%) and suggests undervaluation in my given the market leading position of UNH in the sector.

Other risks and final thoughts

Besides the above fundamental considerations, UNH’s recent technical trading patterns also exhibit several textbook signals for an imminent break out. To start, the stock has successfully overtaken its 200-day moving average (MA) of $308.88 as seen, which itself has ended its long-term downward trajectory and began flattening. These signals suggest to me that traders are becoming increasingly more bullish and are ready to push for another up leg. For this push, I consider the $360 level to be the most likely target given its role of the ceiling during a lengthen consolation period during 2025.

Last but not least, the most bullish sign in my mind is the widening of the Bollinger Bandwidth (BB) as you can tell clearly from the chart. The current share price of $316 is now riding the upper band, signaling powerful upward momentum. At the time of my last writing, the stock prices were largely range bound between a relatively narrow BB. With the recent price rallies, the low and upper BB both began to widen (currently around $257 and $316), opening up more room for further rallies.

There are certainly plenty of downside risks ongoing too. To stay on point, I will just detail two of the downside risks that are most relevant to my above analysis. The first one is the potential of a structural topline stagnation or even a decline. If you recall, my first chart highlights the margin expansion potential judging by the latest earnings revisions. But it is also important for me to point out that these revisions also suggest significant top-line headwind. The revenue projection of $109 for Q1 2026 represents a sequential decline of about 3.5% and no year-over-year growth at all (its topline was also $119 billion for FQ1 2025). Second, the boost from Medicare Advantage rate could be countered by many other factors. A notable factor on my list is the medical utilization rates, especially among senior population. This factor is ultimately unpredictable in my view and has historically fluctuated widely.

To close, this UNH revisit details my thoughts on three new developments since my last writing: its upcoming FQ1 ER, Medicare Advantage’s rate change, and its latest technical trading patterns. There are certainly downside risks. After examining these developments and the stock’s current valuation multiples, I believe the upsides outweigh the downsides. I see large odds for the share price to reach $360 in the near term. Even at $360, the stock’s FWD P/E would only be around 20x, still quite reasonable for a business with UNH’s market positioning.
